title: Increasing nationwide disparities in road freight emissions across cities

data_scope.spatial_coverage: 3,107 US counties, aggregated to Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs)

core_conclusion: Across 3,107 US counties (2011-2020), road-freight emissions of PM10, PM2.5, CO2 and NOx grew more spatially unequal. Two engines of inequity emerge: demand-oriented (cities near freight corridors/terminals bear disproportionate emissions relative to their freight demand, discrepancies rising 5.6-14.2%) and SES-oriented (minority communities along freight networks correlate with higher emissions and steeper household-income decline). The authors urge coordinated national, regional and city action to embed environmental justice into transport planning.
why_it_matters: Makes the hidden localized air-pollution externality of e-commerce and globalized freight spatially explicit at national scale; separates who causes freight demand from who breathes its exhaust; links pollution to economic decline; tracks a worsening decade-long trend tied to SDGs 3, 10 and 11.
why_nature: Nationwide, multi-pollutant, multi-year coverage rather than a single case; couples emissions geography, freight infrastructure and socioeconomic decline into one equity narrative; offers distinct policy levers via demand-oriented vs SES-oriented decomposition; reproducible spatial statistics on open federal data with archived code and data.
added_value_to_my_research: Downscale NEI county totals with GPS/mobile-phone fusion -> hyperlocal spatiotemporal exposure surfaces
MGWR + SHAP on emissions-equity -> map spatially non-stationary mechanisms of injustice
Demand-emissions gap -> multi-objective freight routing/siting optimizer extending EV equity-efficiency work
Chain emissions field to 2SFCA/R2SFCA -> double-jeopardy maps of high-exposure, care-poor communities
Physics-informed spatiotemporal graph -> forecast widening disparities under NEV/rerouting scenarios
